What Mu is: Explain in plain terms that Mu is one agent for the everyday internet — you ask it in plain language and it handles news, mail, search, weather, markets and video for you, instead of an app and a tab for each. Make it concrete: show the kind of thing a person would ask.
Owning your everyday internet: The big platforms own a service for everything and sit at the centre of what you do. Mu is the same everyday stack, but open source and self-hostable as a single Go binary, so a person can run it themselves instead of renting each piece. Be honest that self-hosting is a real, optional path, not a requirement.
Pay for the tools, not your attention: Describe what it feels like to use a service with no ads, no tracking, and no algorithmic feed — finite, calm, no infinite scroll. Tie it to the principle: you pay for the tools, your attention isn't the product.
Built on Go Micro: Explain that every capability in Mu is a real go-micro service and the assistant is a go-micro agent over them, reachable over REST, MCP and A2A. Explain why that architecture is what makes Mu ownable and self-hostable. Do not claim performance numbers or benchmarks.
One question, many services: Show how the agent answers a single question by calling several services on your behalf — e.g. weather plus news plus markets — and composing one answer. Keep it concrete and grounded in what the agent actually does.
Run your own Mu: Explain what self-hosting a single Go binary gives you that a hosted platform never can: your data stays yours, no account required, local models if you want them. Be honest about it being for people who want to run their own.
Answers anchored to today: Explain that Mu treats date-sensitive questions as date-sensitive: the agent anchors answers to the request date and should say when it is using current search, news, weather or market data. Make this about trust and avoiding stale answers, not about claiming perfection.
Search that answers the question: Describe the recent refinement where web search synthesis is grounded in the user's actual query intent, so Mu does not just dump links or generic summaries; it reads the result in light of what was asked and gives a concise answer with caveats when the web is uncertain.
Live news, not cached guesses: Explain that news search is grounded in the live feed rather than model memory, so a person asking about current events gets a fresher, more accountable answer. Be honest that feeds can be incomplete and provider outages should degrade gracefully.
Market answers without the detour: Describe the recent refinement where market-mover questions are planned more directly, so a guest asking what is moving in markets gets to the market data faster instead of waiting through unnecessary planning. Keep it about reducing friction in the ask-to-answer loop, not benchmark claims.
Weather on the first ask: Describe the recent guest-weather refinement: a visitor can ask for weather and Mu should get to a useful forecast quickly, with graceful wording when location or provider data is missing. Make it about first-run usefulness and honest failures.
Result first, caveats after: Describe the recent fallback-answer refinement: when Mu can answer from available tool output, it should lead with the result in the first sentence and put caveats or missing-provider notes after the useful answer. Make this about respecting a person's time, not pretending the system is perfect.
Follow-ups stay in the same thread: Describe the recent conversation-continuity refinement: follow-up questions in the agent experience should stay attached to the same conversation across a page reload, so a person can clarify or narrow an answer without starting over. Keep it concrete and modest.
Fallback sources with labels: Describe the recent AI-news fallback refinements: when the primary news provider is unavailable, Mu falls back to web results, prefers concrete article-like sources over generic topic pages, keeps limited-evidence labels when the source is only a category/root page, and shows readable URLs. Make it about trust in current answers, not pretending fallback evidence is as strong as live news.
Freshness before headlines: Describe the recent news-answer refinement where, if Mu only finds older news for a date-sensitive request, it puts the freshness caveat before the list of stories instead of burying it after the answer. Keep the claim modest: this helps a person know when results are not same-day and avoids presenting stale items as current.
Hold the first words until the news is c: Describe the recent latest-news refinement where Mu holds back the start of a streamed answer once a news/headline tool is in use, so the freshness guard can run before the person sees a confident-sounding current-events answer. Keep it modest: this protects trust in date-sensitive answers; it does not make feeds complete or perfect.
Guarded answers survive replay: Describe the recent native-news streaming refinement where Mu now replays the freshness-guarded answer text, not an earlier unguarded draft, when a news answer is finalized or reconnected. Keep it concrete: for date-sensitive news, the first visible answer should preserve the stale-results caveat instead of sounding current by accident. Be honest that this protects wording around available evidence; it does not make feeds complete or guarantee same-day coverage.
The start screen is the agent: Describe the recent home refinement where Mu's start screen is centered on the same ask-to-answer loop as the product: a prompt first, inline answers, and a personal glance below it rather than a separate dashboard to manage. Keep it concrete and modest: the work reduces the distance between arriving and asking, not that every service is perfect.
Current news should keep its subject: Describe the recent AI-news search refinement where Mu preserves the user's specific topic and recency intent when looking for current news, instead of broadening into generic headlines. Make it about trust: date-sensitive answers should be fresh, but also about the thing the person actually asked for.
Places are part of the everyday stack: Explain that places lookup is now treated as a first-class service the agent can use alongside search, weather, markets and news: useful for everyday questions about where something is, what is nearby, or what location data means. Be honest that provider coverage depends on configuration, with Google Places when configured and open data fallbacks otherwise.
